does: This paper introduces WIMHF, a method that leverages sparse autoencoders to automatically extract and interpret human preferences from feedback datasets without pre-specifying which attributes to study. The approach characterizes both what preferences each dataset can measure and what preferences annotators actually express. By analyzing seven datasets including Reddit conversations, HH-RLHF, PRISM, and LMArena, the authors identify sparse, interpretable features that explain the majority of preference prediction signal. The results reveal substantial diversity in human preferences across contexts—for instance, Reddit users favor informal and humorous responses while HH-RLHF annotators penalize them—and importantly surface potentially problematic preferences like LMArena users voting against model refusals.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that human feedback data significantly influences language model behavior in ways practitioners don't fully understand. It extends prior research studying specific preference attributes like response length, toxicity, or sycophancy by developing an unsupervised method to discover salient preferences automatically. The use of sparse autoencoders follows recent advances in mechanistic interpretability for decomposing neural network representations into human-understandable concepts. The paper responds to limitations in black-box analysis of preference data and connects to broader concerns about alignment and RLHF reliability, building on critical examinations of what training signals actually optimize for in large language models.
enables: This work enables more transparent and auditable approaches to preference learning, allowing practitioners to understand what their feedback data actually encodes before training. The interpretable feature discovery process supports safer deployment of RLHF systems by surfacing unexpected or problematic preference patterns early. The method opens pathways for curating and filtering feedback datasets based on explicitly identified preferences, improving alignment practices. Future work could extend WIMHF to detect conflicting preferences across annotators, identify distributional mismatches between feedback sources, or develop automated tools to remove or correct unsafe preferences from training data. This interpretability foundation could ultimately support more principled and controlled approaches to incorporating human preferences into increasingly capable models.
